data.title: Moody's Vs. S&P Global: AI Risk, Refinancing Boom & 2026 Outlook
data.description:
Follow Luca Socci on Seeking Alpha!

Read Luca Socci's Article on Seeking Alpha!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

Nicole Benjamin: Hey, everybody. It's Nicole Benjamin, your host here at Seeking Alpha to bring to you another episode of Portfolio Pulse, where, as the name suggests, we're keeping a pulse to all the big financial moves happening in the market. Now, for today's episode, we're going to be diving deep into all things MCO and SPGI. And to join us and help us do that today, I have Luca Socci. So Luca, thank you so much for joining us today.

Luca Socci: Oh, thank you for inviting me and having me over. It's a pleasure and an honor.

NB: Yes. Absolutely. So, I see you've been on Seeking Alpha since 2022. So, tell us a little bit more about your financial background, your investment philosophy. What got you on the platform in the first place?

LS: I began my Seeking Alpha journey a bit before 2022. That's when I became an analyst, but before being an analyst, I was actually an investor, a retail investor. And I actually got more focused on investing when I got married and I had kids because I started thinking about how to build wealth for them too. So, basically I started investing because I want to leave them something when they'll be needing it.

NB: Now, I want to jump right in. You recently wrote an article on MCO and SPGI. And in your article, you talk about how Moody's is safer from AI because its models are required by law. So, with that in mind and Moody's 97% retention on AI, do you think this AI fear is overblown?

LS: Mostly, yes. The real fear, the real AI threat that investors think can impact on Moody's and S&P Global is data commoditization. That means that if you use an LLM today, you'll see that compared to a few years ago, they've become a lot better at pulling data from raw PDF files. Okay, so, investors think, why do I need to pay S&P Global or Moody's investing platforms to retrieve data that I can easily pull with my new agent that I can craft myself? And that is partly true, okay, but that doesn't understand what Moody's and S&P Global do, okay, because what is very important is not only pulling data, we agree that this is something that AI will do for us, but what we need when we invest is that we need verified data.

We need someone who takes the liability to say, okay, that number is right or wrong. We need someone to be accountable for and that cannot be AI, it needs to be a reality where human beings are part of it. So, that means corporations. In Moody's and S&P Global too, they offer this. They offer verified data.

NB: You also talk about how these companies are counting on a wall of debt to be refinanced in 2026. So, with that in mind, is this single-digit growth, of these interest rates, like a sure thing? Do you think?

LS: Nothing is sure in the investing world as we're seeing every day. And so, there are two things that matter here. The debt wall is sure. I mean, companies need, companies borrowed cheap money in 2020 and 2021. This is a fact. They did that. Okay. In addition, private credit is also growing and private credit has a shorter life cycle, so it needs to be refinanced in fewer years, compared to public debt.

So, this is something short, we have a lot of debt, cheap debt outstanding, it comes due in 2026 and 2027, but 2026 is the big wall. So, the real question is, whether companies will refinance it or not, but most of them do need to refinance them because they don't have the cash to pay it down. They need that money, they need for their life cycle.

So, it doesn't really, so the thing comes to when the companies will decide to do this. And to this extent, a big variable could be the new Fed's share policy. If interest rates will go down, then maybe, some companies may decide to postpone their refinancing and their debt issuance to the second half of the year instead of the first half of the year. So, that's a big variable. But the fact is that a big debt maturity wall is coming in 2026, and this is a big tailwind for both companies.

NB: Now, Luca, I want to ask, S&P Global is having a spin-off of its Mobility. So, with that becoming a higher profit company, and investors considering this for their portfolio, is this divorce, so to speak, really a bargain or is that a sign that the growth is actually slowing down?

LS: It's actually the thing that makes me a bit more bullish on Moody’s right now than S&P Global, because S&P Global has to face the spin-off, which is not really clear on a few variables. For example, we still don't know the distribution ratio. So, we know that S&P Global will separate the Mobility division into separate company and give some shares to its existing shareholders, but we don't know the ratio between existing shares of S&P Global as it is now and the spin-off.

So, that's something that matters. But also, I think many investors fear this spin-off because they will end up owning shares of the new S&P Global with ex-Mobility and the Mobility company, which being linked to the automotive world, it's a car data business, so to speak. Investors fear that it will be given lower multiples, compared to the whole S&P Global as it is now.

NB: So, Moody's is aiming for a 53% profit margin, which is honestly huge in comparison to other industries. So, I guess with that in mind, you said, you're bullish on it. Does this – what is it in their business model that allows them to get 50% on every dollar that they're making?

LS: Oh, there are three things. Low marginal cost, almost no competition on price, and mandatory demand. When you rate debt, the only cost you have is very tiny, a team of analysts, a proprietary model you developed and your reputation that you built over a century. So, the cost of issuing debt over and over becomes very lower and lower every time you do that, because there is a low marginal cost. There's also no competition in price because basically Moody's and S&P Global together with Fitch are an oligopoly on debt rating and mandatory demand.

That means that, companies have to get their debt rated if they want to pay the lowest possible interest on that debt. If they don't get the debt rated, they'll pay higher interest. So, it's in their interest to get their debt rated. And this is what makes Moody's business so exceptional.

NB: All right. Well, we'll leave it right there. Thank you so much, Luca, for joining us today. And for everybody that's listening in, go ahead, click the follow button on Luca's page. Go look into Moody's. See if S&P Global is going to be right for your portfolio. And we'll see you here next time on another episode.

Follow Luca Socci on Seeking Alpha!

Read Luca Socci's Article on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Indexing Impact Bonds: Insights Into A Growing And Maturing Market
data.description:
By Alan Meng, Sustainable Fixed Income Product Manager | Claire Hugo, Sustainable Fixed Income Senior Product Specialist

Impact bonds support a wide range of outcomes, from financing climate solutions to supporting social programmes and sustainable development. These bonds, which encompass labelled green, social and sustainability (GSS) bonds[1], have become an increasingly important mechanism for enabling investors to direct capital towards their environmental and social objectives.

By the end of 2025, the total outstanding global GSS bond market had reached $5.32trn, comprising $3.3trn in green bonds, $827bn in social bonds and $1.2trn in sustainability bonds. For many investors, impact bonds are now a core component of global fixed income portfolios.

As impact bond issuance accelerates, investors face growing challenges around transparency and comparability across this rapidly expanding market. In this FTSE Russell Insight, we examine key market trends and how the FTSE Impact Bond Index Series provides comprehensive, standards-aligned coverage of this important fixed income market segment.

Growth and resilience across the impact bond market

The labelled GSS bond market has expanded significantly in the last decade, with annual issuance increasing more than sixfold since 2016 and green bonds consistently accounting for the majority of primary market volumes (Figure 1).

Despite a more complex macroeconomic backdrop in recent years, the labelled bond market has remained resilient. In 2025, annual issuance continued to grow, with total GSS issuance reaching $856bn—representing 4% year-on-year growth and 6.5% of overall new bond issuance.[2] Among the GSS total, green bonds continued to anchor the market, with $573bn issued in 2025, compared to $204bn in sustainability bonds and $79bn in social bonds.

Regionally, despite a modest year-on-year decline, Europe remained the largest source of green bond issuance in 2025 (at $311bn, representing 54% of global volumes). Issuance in the region has been supported by ongoing regulatory developments, such as the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation 2.0 and the EU Green Bond Standard.

In 2025, Asia-Pacific emerged as the fastest-growing region for impact bonds, with issuance rising 30%, driven in part by a near-doubling of green bond issuance in China from the year before. This trend reflects strengthening regulatory drivers, such as Japan’s GX transition policy and China’s updated green taxonomy, alongside rising investor appetite across these markets.

Overall, the impact bond market’s ability to sustain growth amid geopolitical tensions and broader market volatility highlights continued investor demand and the increasing role of labelled bonds in global fixed income allocations.

Figure 1. Annual issuance of labelled GSS bonds

Broadening applications of impact bond financing

This sustained growth is also helping to expand the types of projects financed through impact bond issuance. As shown in Figure 2, the proceeds of GSS bond issues now support a wide spectrum of initiatives—from renewable energy and clean transport to affordable housing, healthcare and education—reflecting both evolving sustainability priorities and the maturation of the labelled bond market.

Climate mitigation remains a core focus in the impact bond market, with energy efficiency accounting for 13% of total GSS bond proceeds by outstanding amount and renewable energy an additional 11%. Social themes continue to expand, with socioeconomic advancement and empowerment representing the largest share of social-related proceeds.

Beyond these categories, the use-of-proceeds universe is continuing to evolve, reflecting demand for financing tools that address emerging sustainability challenges while diversifying portfolio exposure. For example, the digital infrastructure supporting energy and water efficiency, nature-based solutions—particularly in emerging markets—and climate adaptation are gaining traction.

Figure 2. Use-of-proceeds breakdown of the global green, social and sustainability bonds market in 2025

Supported by the 2025 International Capital Market Association (ICMA) Climate Transition Bond Guidelines, which clarify what constitutes credible transition activities, the impact bond market is now better positioned for a more diversified use of issuance proceeds. This includes greater financing across hard-to-abate sectors such as steel, cement, aviation and shipping, in emerging markets, where transition needs are accelerating, and among financial institutions aiming to reduce financed emissions.

Benchmarking the impact bond market

As the market matures and diversifies, investor expectations are also rising. There is progressively greater scrutiny of the impact bond market, particularly with regards to the transparency and standards associated with impact bond issuance and the accountability for the use of proceeds. This is placing increased emphasis on the integrity of labelled bonds within sustainable fixed income portfolios.

By applying clear eligibility criteria and ongoing monitoring, impact bond benchmarks provide a consistent, rules-based framework that enhances comparability and supports accountability. They help investors navigate differences in disclosure, classifications and use of proceeds, ensuring that labelled bonds meet recognised standards.

To support investors in this rapidly evolving market, FTSE Russell has developed the FTSE Impact Bond Index Series—a comprehensive suite of benchmarks covering GSS bonds with transparency, comparability and consistent classification (Figure 3).

Figure 3. FTSE Impact Bond Index Series

A key differentiator of the index series is its focus on methodological rigour, transparent rules and alignment with leading market standards. Green bonds are identified using the Climate Bonds Initiative’s (CBI’s) screening criteria to ensure the credibility and robustness of their green credentials, while social and sustainability bonds must comply with ICMA Principles and Guidelines.

Social and sustainability bonds are also subject to validation at both issuance and post-issuance stages, with ongoing monitoring to confirm that proceeds are allocated as intended—reinforcing accountability and helping to strengthen market trust.

The indices also enable investors to track the performance of labelled bonds (Figure 4). Historically, impact bond segments have performed broadly in line with conventional fixed income markets. However, it is noteworthy that the relative performance of impact bond portfolios has generally been more sensitive to interest rate increases, due to these bonds’ higher active duration profiles, which in turn is underpinned by a higher concentration of long-duration government green bonds.

Figure 4. FTSE Impact Bond Index Series performance

As the labelled bond market continues to evolve, credible and transparent benchmarks are essential. Explore the FTSE Impact Bond Index Series to see how benchmarks can support standards-aligned integration of the labelled bond markets. Access the solution overview, review the index ground rules, or contact our team to discuss how these benchmarks can support your sustainable fixed income strategies.

Sources

[1] Note: throughout this blog, we use 'labelled bonds', 'GSS bonds' and 'impact bonds' interchangeably to reflect common market usage.

[2] Issuance figures exclude US municipal bonds.

Disclaimer

All rights reserved.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: I Still Believe In The Turn-Around
data.description:
I know that NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) wasn't a successful investment for me. I wrote 4 articles since June 2024. All of them were bullish and I put my money where my mouth was. I invested in NLCP a few times.

Fortunately, I did not make it a material position in my portfolio. Over time, I saw its stock price fall by a lot. Over 26% down since my first article about it.

I could sugar-coat it by saying that dividends reduced the pain, but I'm not that kind of a person. Yes, dividends helped, but they also fell short. You can see in the chart above that the total return was also negative at 9%. So I don't think it was a successful buy. So far.

After a year since my last coverage, I decided to follow-up on NLCP. I still think NLCP has a chance to turn things around. I know it's limited by regulatory things around the cannabis use (adult, medical, etc.), but I think it's just the matter of time. Look at the map below, I believe we will see more of dark blue and green in the next few years.

2025 had challenges for NLCP, but I'm optimistic

I often talk about how important diversification is. But I sometimes invest in less diversified businesses when I think it makes sense. That's my case with NLCP. This is not a diversified REIT. Why do I think so? Just three of its largest tenants hold over 50% of its rental income.

In my last article, I discussed the cases of Calypso and Revolutionary Clinics. I will start with bad news, I mean Revolutionary Clinics, but there's one positive in that. The tenant left, but stayed longer than NLCP expected:

At our Pittsburgh, Massachusetts facility leased to Revolutionary Clinics, the tenant remained in the premises longer than expected, providing NewLake with additional rent through the end of the second quarter. In early July, our tenant vacated the building, and we've been actively marketing the property for re-leasing. There's been some early interest, but given the difficulty of the Massachusetts market, we expect that it will take some time to re-lease the property.

I see that the property is still empty and the vacancy had a negative impact on NLCP's results for 2025. As the management commented during the last Earnings Call:

For the full year of 2025, our portfolio generated total revenue of $51.1 million, representing a modest 1.9% increase from $50.1 million for the full year of 2024. The key factors contributing to this revenue growth include rental income from the 2025 acquisition of 2 Ohio dispensaries, a full year of rent generated from a property that we acquired in 2024 for $4 million, a full year of rent generated from funded improvement allowances during 2024 of $15.1 million and annual rent escalators that consistently boost our revenue.

The increase in revenue was partially offset by the impact of vacancies at 2 properties previously leased to Ayr and 1 property previously leased to Revolutionary Clinics.

But I saw much more positive news about Calypso. That business remains NLCP's tenant and it seems to me that they improved their situation materially. I think NLCP still has them on their radar, but the management shared some positive thoughts in Q1 2025. I'm especially happy that Calypso is paying rent:

With regards to Calypso, their security deposit was applied to rent through mid-February, after which we received the remaining outstanding rent for the first quarter in connection with the financial restructuring of Calypso to raise new third party capital. We're excited for our tenant, and the added capital should allow them to build out own dispensaries, creating a vertically integrated operator in Pennsylvania with the adult use legislation just passed through the state house this week. The Calypso transaction is currently awaiting regulatory approval and customary closing conditions. We expect to receive full rent moving forward.

I was satisfied with these news and transparency around them. In my eyes, 2025 was important. Yes, yes, it didn't bring many positives that would shift the market's sentiment. But I think that NLCP navigates its previous issues well and keeps doing their job to make the portfolio safer. I listened to the management and they said:

Throughout 2025, our team remained focused on mitigating risks across the portfolio, addressing vacancies and sourcing high-quality investment opportunities. That's work that's continued into 2026. During the year, we closed 2 smaller transactions with our existing tenant Cresco Labs, and we partnered with tenants, Curaleaf and C3 to optimize property performance and further reduce long-term risk in the portfolio.

Growth catalysts are still there and the price is attractive

There are two levels to review NLCP's growth catalysts on the regulatory front. State-level and Federal level. I won't go through all the details here, I discussed them before. These steps did not materialize yet, but quarter by quarter, I think we're getting closer. I've NLCP's take on that.

The sector is what makes NLCP attractive in my eyes. The cannabis industry is expected to grow to as much as $54B by 2029. I see that NLCP will be able to benefit from that. Its tenants will have better financial health. They will be able to expand to new states and have more facilities. That means safer and higher rental income for NLCP. Yes, we had some bad votes at state-levels at the end of 2024, but as I said back then - it's not "game-over". I always pay attention to NLCP's presentation, as the team bullets some opportunities ahead of the sector. I'm pasting that below.

These are the reasons why I think NLCP will perform better through 2027 than in 2024 - 2025. I see some ways for NLCP to get higher rental income. Will they drive AFFO growth? I think they will. Especially with changes on the regulatory front. And that's not everything NLCP offers.

I also see that it has a very attractive valuation. Just 7.4x forward P/FFO multiple, which is around half of the multiple non-cannabis REITs typically trade on (according to NLCP's presentation linked earlier). If that's not "cheap", I don't know what is. It trades at even lower multiple than its closest competitor Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), but NLCP has a lower payout ratio. I read it as a lower valuation and safer dividend

That's why I still believe. I think 2026 will be the year we see some recovery and in the mid-term, I expect NLCP to generate high double-digit returns. Dividends are attractive, quite safe, and I think the valuation makes the downside risk limited. I think NLCP is a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Millicom: The New And Better Telefonica Latam
data.description:
Introduction

Upon occasion, as part of my formal position, I come across interesting debt and equity ideas. Millicom (TIGO) has been on a buying spree, acquiring Telefonica’s (TEF) Latam subsidiaries at low valuations with plenty of productivity gains and, more importantly, consolidating markets into two or three players. The market has taken notice; the shares have increased 5x in the last two years, and while this level of return may not repeat, TIGO does offer good upside with increasing dividends for the more risk-averse investor.

What is TIGO

Millicom is an over 40-year-old company originally focused on frontier mobile markets in Asia, Africa, and South America, places many companies shunned due to the complexity of operations and jurisdiction risk. Today, the company is fully focused on Latin America, with over 60 million mobile subscribers and 20 million cable/broadband clients across 11 countries, where it is 1st or 2nd in market share. In 2025, it acquired many of Telefónica's subsidiaries in the region (Colombia, Uruguay, Ecuador, and Chile), adding to the Telefónica assets purchased since 2019. Following the recent M&A, TIGO will have greater business concentration in Colombia, with about 36% of revenue, followed by Guatemala at 21%. In both countries, América Móvil (AMX) is the other main competitor.

Operating Model

The company provides a high degree of operating disclosure, with mobile, cable, and broadband clients per country, as well as revenue and EBITDA. I modeled mobile subscribers and ARPU by country, as well as the fixed-line business, to forecast revenue. I don’t see much room for mobile subscribers' growth in fully penetrated markets, but since about 80% are prepaid, there is room to incentivize a move to postpaid, which generally improves ARPU. Otherwise, ARPU growth should be in line with inflation. The sector is not a top-line growth story.

TIGO has grown its margins through cost reduction and productivity, with fewer plans, lower customer care, and a streamlined supplier base to keep complexity and costs lower. According to conversations with TIGO, they have half the headcount that Telefónica operations normally do. This is why, in 2026, I assume opex will increase, and margins will decline as they restructure the many Telefonica operations recently acquired, especially in Colombia, which is more than twice what they had before. This productivity drive should occur during 2026 and part of 2027. Thus, EBITDA margins should reach 2025 levels by 2028.

Capital Discipline

Another key aspect of TIGO’s investment case is the low capex spend, which, in conjunction with EBITDA margin gains, should drive FCF to over $1.8 billion by 2027. High capex with low returns has been the telecom sector's Achilles heel; every new technology cycle (3G, 4G, etc.) required massive equipment upgrades as well as added spectrum. This increase in capex and debt did not result in higher revenue per user; rather, it led to lower revenue per user due to intense competition. At the same time, the entire internet ecosystem surfed, at no cost, on the telecoms' dumb pipes due to net neutrality. This low capex is due to TIGO’s view that 5G only needs to be built in key high-density locations rather than for national coverage, and also to advances in compression technology that reduce data transmission loads. AI is computationally intensive in data centers and does not require additional or larger data transmission capacity.

Valuation

TIGO shares are no longer cheap; this strong performance is driven by higher margins and FCF growth, as well as anticipation of significant value extraction from the recent M&A. The consensus price target of $73 implies an EV/EBITDA multiple of 6x based on consensus forecasts. The sector average is 5.5x for 2026, but most telecoms have lower growth and FCF due to higher costs and capex spending. In my view, TIGO could trade at a premium of 6.5x EV/EBITDA, providing low upside to YE26 but over 40% potential to YE27.

Peer Comps

The telecom sector is extensive, and I selected some of the larger companies worldwide, along with their Latin American peers. On a revenue and EBITDA growth basis, TIGO stands out, and its capex levels as a percentage of EBITDA are lower than most, which further supports a premium valuation vs. peers. The whole sector has been re-rated as investors rotated from software, which is under AI threats, towards more resilient and predictable cash flow and capital returns.

Risk

Operating in emerging markets carries higher currency and macro risk. The majority of TIGO's revenue is derived in local currencies, which can be subject to devaluation and lower USD earnings, which can impact the stock price. Weaker macro has far less impact, as data services, whether via mobile or fixed, are as important as water and electricity. Increased competition is the greatest risk, leading to lost customers, lower revenue, and margin squeeze. This usually occurs when one player has under 10% market share and needs to increase its revenue base to meet fixed costs.

Conclusion

I rate Millicom a hold. More conservative investors may be better served by holding TIGO shares, given its resilient business from oil shocks, inflation, and AI threats, and focus on increasing FCF and dividends. Capital appreciation is a 2027 probability given the already premium valuation.

data.title: Aehr Test Systems: Strong Prospects But Fully Valued - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Aehr Test Systems, Inc., or "Aehr" (AEHR) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Aehr Test Systems Overview

After the close of Tuesday's regular session, aspiring semiconductor test and burn-in system supplier Aehr Test Systems reported another set of less-than-stellar quarterly results with revenues coming in below consensus expectations and persistent cash burn:

While non-GAAP gross margin of 36.5% recovered from multi-year lows below 30% experienced in the preceding quarter, the replacement of high-margin silicon carbide wafer-level burn-in system sales with lower-margin package-level burn-in products continues to negatively impact profitability.

Aehr burned $3.8 million in cash during the third quarter. Similar to Q2, the company used the ongoing AI hype to replenish its dwindling cash balances by selling shares at elevated prices into the open market.

During the quarter, the company raised gross proceeds of $10.5 million from sales under its up to $40 million Open Market Sale Agreement with Jefferies LLC (JEF).

Subsequent to quarter-end, Aehr sold additional shares into the open market for gross proceeds of $19.5 million thus exhausting the Open Market Sale Agreement.

However, weak financial results and persistent dilution were more than offset by the company's stellar bookings performance as also celebrated by management in the press release:

We are very pleased with the strong momentum in our business across multiple market segments, highlighted by more than $37 million in quarterly bookings and a book-to-bill ratio exceeding 3.5x.

Demand continues to accelerate across both WLBI and PLBI as semiconductor devices grow in size, complexity, and power and are increasingly deployed in mission-critical AI, networking, automotive, and industrial applications.

Subsequent to quarter-end, Aehr booked an additional $12.2 million in orders. As a result, effective backlog increased to a record $50.9 million.

During Q3, the company received a $14 million follow-on production order from its undisclosed lead wafer-level AI accelerator processor customer for multiple new fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in systems which will be used in data center training and inference applications.

For the first time in recent quarters, Aehr managed to secure a follow-on order from its lead silicon photonics customer ON Semiconconductor Corporation (ON) with additional orders expected going into 2027.

Perhaps most importantly, the company was awarded a large purchase order from its lead hyperscale customer which is widely believed to be Amazon (AMZN) for package-level burn-in of the customer’s next-generation AI processor.

The initial order includes multiple Aehr Sonoma™ ultra-high-power package-level test and burn-in systems for production burn-in of this next-generation device.

Delivery of these systems, along with the fully turnkey burn-in modules (BIMs) and device-specific sockets required for this next generation ASIC AI processor, is scheduled for summer of 2026.

In addition, the customer is forecasting a very large expansion of Sonoma system purchases for its current generation AI processor in H2/2026 and going into 2027.

As a result, the company has signed an agreement with one of its contract manufacturers for the production of more than 20 additional Sonoma systems per month.

Aehr also reported progress with its lead Gallium Nitride ("GaN") customer which is assumed to be Infineon Technologies (IFNNY, IFNNF).

However, not everything is going according to plan as the company's benchmark evaluation program with a top-tier AI processor supplier has been experiencing delays due to "a technical misunderstanding on the clock configurations" as outlined by management on the conference call.

Moreover, it will take more time and development work for the company to win business from memory suppliers with initial shipments of multi-wafer test and burn-in systems currently not expected before FY2028.

Lastly, the EV silicon carbide market remains in poor condition. Consequently, management does not expect the segment to contribut significant revenue in the near future.

With strong second half bookings so far and additional orders expected until the end of the company's fiscal year in May, Aehr now expects full-year revenues to come in at the high-end of the $45 million to $50 million range indicated last quarter. In addition, bookings are projected to also reach the high-end of the $60 million to $80 million range stated in the Q2/2026 earnings release.

While the initial after hours reaction was muted, the combination of an analyst upgrade and a surging market resulted in the company's shares rallying by more than 25% to new all-time highs on Wednesday:

However, Craig-Hallum's upgrade should be taken with a grain of salt as the company's new $68 price target is based on expectations for annual revenues exceeding $200 million at some point going forward and resulting earnings per share of $1.70 to $1.80.

Quite frankly, even when assuming a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 40% it would take until the next decade for Aehr to reach these sales levels.

Please note also that Craig-Hallum is a sales agent under the company's new $60 million Equity Distribution Agreement.

AEHR Stock Valuation

Given the company's mediocre track record, I am hesitant using aggressive multi-year growth assumptions.

But even when basing my price target on very optimistic FY2028 profitability estimates and an industry-leading EV/EBITDA multiple of 35x, the stock appears fully-valued.

However, with shares price having broken out to new all-time highs on massive trading volume, I am hesitant to fight momentum.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on Aehr Test Systems' common stock.

Bottom Line

While Aehr Test Systems Q3/FY2026 earnings report was nothing to write home about, a recent surge in system orders has resulted in backlog increasing to new all-time highs.

As a result, FY2027 should be a year of strong revenue growth for the company. However, with package-level burn-in products expected to contribute the majority of sales for the time being, Aehr's gross margins are not likely to recover to previous levels anytime soon.

Given the projected surge in revenues, the company will have to prepare for substantial working capital investments. As a result, I would expect Aehr Test Systems to continue selling shares into the open market under its new $60 million Equity Distribution Agreement.

That said, even when assuming the company finally starting to deliver on its promises, the stock remains fully-valued at current levels.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on Aehr Test Systems' shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sector Performance Drivers: Why Mega-Cap Exposure Matters
data.description:
Traditional sector ETFs may not fully capture the mega-cap companies that drive sector returns, as regulatory diversification caps can limit exposure to the largest holdings.

The Role of Mega-Cap Companies in Sector Returns

The U.S. equity market looks very different than it did even five years ago. A small group of mega-cap companies now account for an outsized share of their sectors' market capitalization, earnings, and returns.

The technology sector is the clearest example. As of early 2026, tech represents over 43% of the S&P 500's total market capitalization and roughly 36% of its earnings. Within the sector, the concentration is even more lopsided: NVIDIA, Apple, and Microsoft make up a huge portion of the information technology sector's total weight. In consumer discretionary, Amazon and Tesla hold similarly dominant positions, and in communication services, Alphabet and Meta Platforms tower over the rest.

This isn't a coincidence. These companies are the engines of sector performance. In 2025, information technology and communication services together accounted for over 63% of the S&P 500's total return. Without those two sectors, the index would have returned roughly 6%.

For investors who want targeted sector exposure, the takeaway is simple: capturing the performance of these leading companies isn't optional. Any sector strategy that systematically underweights them is going to face meaningful performance drag.

How RIC Diversification Caps Distort Sector Exposure

Most ETFs in the United States are structured as Regulated Investment Companies (RICs) under the Internal Revenue Code. This structure lets funds pass through income and gains to shareholders without paying corporate-level tax. But to qualify, RICs must meet quarterly diversification tests, commonly known as the 25/5/50 rule:

  • No single company can represent more than 25% of the fund's total assets.
  • All positions exceeding 5% each cannot, in aggregate, exceed 50% of the fund.

These rules exist to protect investors from excessive concentration risk. For broadly diversified portfolios, they're a non-issue. But in today's concentrated sectors, they create real distortions.

Look at technology. In an uncapped, true market-cap representation of the tech sector, NVIDIA and Microsoft alone can account for over 40% of the sector's weight. RIC rules don't allow that. So a traditional tech sector ETF has to scale those names back and push that weight into smaller companies that contribute far less to sector performance.

What does this mean in practice? Investors who buy a traditional technology sector ETF may think they're getting full, representative exposure to the sector. But they're actually holding a portfolio that's been reshaped by regulatory constraints, one that underweights the companies driving returns and overweights names with a smaller economic footprint.

Sector-by-Sector Breakdown: Where Capping Hurts Most

The distortion from RIC caps isn't the same everywhere. It hits hardest in sectors where a few large names make up the bulk of the market cap.

Information Technology: Tech is the most affected sector. Names like NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, and Broadcom command a huge share of the sector's market cap. Capping forces traditional sector funds into a structural underweight relative to these companies' true market-cap weight. On the surface the portfolio looks like the tech sector; underneath, it behaves quite differently.

Consumer Discretionary: Mega-cap e-commerce and retail names, most notably Amazon and Tesla, drive the majority of this sector's performance. Under capping rules, their influence gets diluted, and exposure shifts toward smaller retailers and consumer companies that contribute less to overall returns.

Communication Services: A small number of streaming, social media, and digital advertising companies dominate this sector, including Alphabet, Meta Platforms, and Netflix. When these names get capped, benchmark alignment breaks down, introducing performance drag and unintended tilts toward less impactful holdings.

Across all five sectors, the pattern is the same: the companies that investors most want exposure to are exactly the ones that regulatory caps force funds to underweight.

Tracking Error and What It Means for Sector Investors

The practical cost of RIC-driven capping is tracking error, or the volatility of the gap between a sector fund's performance and that of an uncapped sector benchmark, many investors often follow.

For asset allocators and model portfolio managers, tracking error is more than a technical footnote. It chips away at the precision of portfolio construction. If a sector allocation is meant to express a specific market view (say, a conviction in technology's growth trajectory), the effectiveness of that view depends on the fund closely mirroring the actual makeup of the sector.

Traditional capped sector ETFs often fall short here. Redistributing weight away from mega-cap leaders and into smaller names introduces stock-level biases and performance differences that compound over time. What looks like a clean sector bet may carry embedded distortions that quietly erode its effectiveness.

For investors who care about attribution clarity and precise exposure, reducing tracking error relative to uncapped benchmarks is a real, tangible improvement.

The Case for True Market-Cap Sector Investing

If the goal of sector investing is to capture the economic reality of a market segment, then the approach should reflect how that sector actually exists in the marketplace, not an artificially capped version of it.

True market-cap sector exposure means holding each company at its natural weight, as the market determines it. When NVIDIA represents a significant share of the technology sector's value, the portfolio should reflect that rather than capping it at an arbitrary threshold and redistributing the difference into smaller positions.

This matters for several practical reasons. It produces cleaner attribution, since returns tie more directly to true sector dynamics rather than capping’s unanticipated consequences. It cuts down on unintended stock-level biases, making sector investing more precise. And it aligns the portfolio with the actual performance drivers of the market instead of a regulatory approximation of them.

As sectors have become more concentrated, fueled by the scale advantages and AI-driven growth of mega-cap tech and platform companies, the gap between capped and uncapped sector exposure has only widened. For allocators building portfolios around sector views, closing that gap matters more now than it used to.

Accessing Precise Sector Exposure with VanEck TruSector ETFs

VanEck's TruSector ETFs were built to address this structural problem. The suite currently includes five actively managed ETFs targeting the sectors most affected by capping distortions:

  • VanEck Technology TruSector ETF (TRUT): Full market-cap exposure to the information technology sector.
  • VanEck Consumer Discretionary TruSector ETF (TRUD): Full market-cap exposure to the consumer discretionary sector.
  • VanEck Communication Services TruSector ETF (TRUC): Full market-cap exposure to the communication services sector.
  • VanEck Financials TruSector ETF (TRUF): Full market-cap exposure to the financials sector.
  • VanEck Healthcare TruSector ETF (TRUH): Full market-cap exposure to the healthcare sector.

Each fund uses a hybrid structure that combines direct equity holdings with positions in targeted sector ETFs. The funds hold individual sector stocks up to the maximum allowed by RIC rules, then pick up supplemental exposure through other sector ETFs. Because RIC diversification rules don't "look through" to the underlying holdings of other RICs, those positions do not count toward issuer concentration rules. The result is funds that can offer the economic exposure similar to an uncapped sector benchmark while staying fully RIC compliant.

In practice, this means TruSector ETFs maintain uncapped exposure to each sector's leading contributors (or detractors), avoid the overallocation to smaller names that may plague traditional sector funds, and deliver tighter tracking relative to widely followed benchmarks, all with the operational simplicity and liquidity that ETF investors expect.

For asset allocators who want to express sector views with greater accuracy, VanEck's TruSector ETFs offer a sharper tool for portfolio construction in a market that keeps getting more concentrated.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 widely held common stocks covering the leading industries of the U.S. economy.

Index returns are not Fund returns and do not reflect any management fees or brokerage expenses. Certain indices may take into account withholding taxes. Investors can not invest directly in the Index. Returns for actual Fund investors may differ from what is shown because of differences in timing, the amount invested and fees and expenses. Index returns assume that dividends have been reinvested.

Fund Holdings may vary. Visit TRUT - VanEck Technology TruSector ETF - Holdings for a complete list of holdings.

Fund Holdings may vary. Visit TRUD - VanEck Consumer Discretionary TruSector ETF - Holdings for a complete list of holdings.

Fund Holdings may vary. Visit TRUC - VanEck Communication Services TruSector ETF - Holdings for a complete list of holdings.

Fund Holdings may vary. Visit TRUF - VanEck Financials TruSector ETF - Holdings for a complete list of holdings.

Fund Holdings may vary. Visit TRUH - VanEck Healthcare TruSector ETF - Holdings for a complete list of holdings.

An investment in the Funds may be subject to risks which include, among others, risks related to investing in the communication services sector, consumer discretionary sector, information technology sector, healthcare sector, financials sector, software industry, derivatives, equity securities, investing in other ETFs, investment restrictions, issuer-specific changes, medium- and large-capitalization companies, market, operational, active management, authorized participant concentration, seed investor, new fund, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, fund shares trading, premium/discount and liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified and concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Funds. Medium- and large-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com/etfs. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

© Van Eck Securities Corporation, Distributor, a wholly owned subsidiary of Van Eck Associates Corporation.

data.title: Climb Global Solutions: Market Waiting To See What AI Means (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I first wrote about Climb (CLMB) in the spring of 2024 - that article offers an introduction to the company's business model and corporate strategy. At the time, Climb was experiencing mid-teens organic growth, and management had outlined a strategy - funded by cash - to turbocharge that growth through acquisitions. As I argued then, the market was underestimating the likelihood of that strategy's success. Hence the article's title:

Climb Global Solutions: Cash Can Fund Acquisitions And Double Earnings By 2026

That thesis largely played out over the course of 2025, with shares more than doubling from ~$15 (post-4-1-split) to over $30. Over the last few months, after a recent article where I reiterated my bullish call, shares have given back most of those gains. My October, 2025 article has not aged well, with shares down around 25% since the call.

Two quarters of results have come in and disappointed investors. But since the market is forward-looking, in this article I will focus not so much on the specific results of those two quarters as in what they tell us about Climb's prospects going forward. The most important question for Climb today is how AI will influence (1) demand for the software their vendors sell and (2) their own moat as a software distributor. Climb's margins and billings growth have both disappointed over the last six months, and this has obviously weighed on shares. But the multiples have also compressed significantly as part of the broader SaaS sell-off. Is that sell-off warranted? At the most basic level, it is too early to tell, and that creates uncertainty, which should lead us to discount shares. This article outlines a series of questions to guide investors as we assess results over the next 12 months.

I think shares are probably oversold at current levels, but given the weakening recent results and uncertainty over the future, I'm downgrading shares to a hold (do not initiate). Below, I review recent results based on the Q3 and Q4 earnings PR and call transcripts, which are available from Seeking Alpha.

Q4 Results

Revenue beat in Q4, but adjusted EBITDA and gross profit both declined YOY, and the stock sold off hard. Management said that this was due to quarterly lumpiness - the impact of a single very large transaction in Q4 2024 created a hard comp. VAST Data, one of Climb's largest vendors, had a major transaction in Q4 2024 with an earn-out that was added back into EBITDA, inflating the number in a way that did not reflect underlying organic growth. Stripping out the effect of this one-time charge, according to management, Q4 2025 revenue and billings both grow "in the high teens." But this was bad news. It solidified a trend of gross margin compression over all four quarters of 2025 from about 20% to about 16%. Given that Climb's margins have always been thin, this is a huge problem for the company's profitability. They also suspended the dividend, which drove away some investors who had appreciated the yield. I personally did not care about the dividend, which was very small, and see this as irrelevant to the stock's total return profile, but it matters for the market for shares.

The bigger and more important question is, why the margin compression?

Will AI Competition Hurt Climb's Vendors? Time Will Tell

On the Q3 and Q4 earnings calls, management cited a number of specific factors contributing to the hit to margins. After SolarWinds, one of their largest vendors, was bought out in 2024, the new owners adjusted the pricing model, causing some disruption for Climb. Climb also lost a vendor, Citrix, creating a ~$50m hole. Climb was able to make up for this with other new vendors, but perhaps that came at the expense of margins. More generally, management claimed that the difference in Q4 was an accounting one: they said that more of their product sales under the current vendor mix are being recognized on a gross basis, with the effect of driving up billings faster than gross profit or EBITDA and compressing margins artificially. But this is not a very convincing explanation, in my view, because it doesn't actually explain why EBITDA is flat. The combination of (1) tough comps, (2) accounting changes, and (3) product mix shift together explain the rough quarter, but they don't get the company off the hook. The bottom line is that margins have been under pressure and growth has been decelerating. This is bad news, and begs for a more global explanation.

For that, I think we need to turn to what's happening in the market for software more generally. As you surely know, the conversation over the last few months has been dominated by the idea that AI is eating software. The cost of writing code has plummeted, and the stocks of all sorts of SaaS providers have come under extreme pressure. So, our first real explanation for the stock sell-off is just industry sentiment shift. But more substantively, I think the first concern here is that the price of software is just coming under pressure generally as more companies are able to vibe code their own solutions or buy from just-out-of-the-box AI-powered start-ups. I am not so worried about this particular possibility because many of Climb's large vendors are in the cybersecurity and cloud storage/data management segments, where there are higher barriers to entry.

A second trend that is more immediately concerning is that, in part due to the rise of agentic AI, companies are becoming cheaper with their SaaS budgets and requiring more immediate ROI from their subscriptions. There had been discussion of pressures on enterprise software spending even in 2024, but they have only grown louder in the last 12 months.

The most immediate catalyst for revenue growth will be Climb's partnership with Fortinet, one of the world's largest pure-play security vendors. Climb is currently targeting $250m in Fortinet billings in 18 months, which would represent ~12% growth and thereby potentially increase revenue growth above 20%.

Many of Climb's large and new vendors, such as Darktrace, are "AI-native," and Climb investors might *hope* that they are on the side of the "disruptors" rather than that of the "disrupted." But (1) this is very hard to evaluate, (2) the situation remains quite fluid, and (3) many enterprises are redirecting their software budgets towards their own AI development.

We don't yet know how this will impact Climb. So far, however, the trend is for continued billings growth (indicating that Climb's vendors are competing effectively in this marketplace) with falling gross margins (indicating that, at least for some parts of the market, prices are falling). The bottom line for Climb is lower profitability and heightened uncertainty - bad news.

Will AI Disintermediate Software Vendors? Probably Not

A second-order question is the degree to which AI will enable vendors to circumvent Climb altogether and sell directly to enterprises. The major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) have been pushing their own "marketplace" solutions, effectively entering the market for software distribution.

Despite that possibility, the early signals here have been generally positive. In principle, if AI leads to a proliferation of new kinds of software and new cybersecurity threats, this should make Climb's vendor vetting and brand reputation even more valuable. And there is some early evidence that this is happening. In Q4, Climb signed 2 new vendors from 100 who applied to join the line card - a 100% increase from the average of about 50/quarter in 2023-2025 (see my prior articles for details on this). This suggests that Climb's distribution services are seeing increased demand in an era of AI "eating" software. Competitors TD Synnex (SNX) and Ingram Micro (INGM) have also seen strong revenue growth in recent quarters. While larger marketplaces and in-house software development are definitely risks to watch for, at this point I see relatively little evidence that Climb is at risk of getting disintermediated.

Climb's AI Strategy

In the midst of this discussion about "AI eating software," it's easy to forget that software companies like Climb aren't sitting still. In Q4, Climb announced its own internal AI initiative to improve processes and margins. This "532" plan aims to increase the gross profit margin to 5%, drive SG&A as a percentage of billings from 2.9% to 2.5%, and enable a further 50% in gross billings growth (approximately a 1-2 year runway) with no additional headcount, which would mean approximately an additional 10-20% increase in EBITDA. Climb hired a new CIO from World Wide Technology to spearhead this effort. I doubt that this would be enough to offset the 350 bps drop in gross margin over the last 12 months, but it is definitely a positive sign.

Additionally, Climb's existing limited-line-card strategy positions it fairly well to adapt to disruption in the SaaS space. Since Climb continually reevaluates vendors based on their sales volumes, evaluating new ones and dropping less successful ones, the company should be able to continue benefiting from AI disruption. While the process itself may be bumpy as some big current vendors lose momentum and new ones ramp up, the change itself is not a threat to Climb's basic model. In that spirit, I view the decision to suspend the dividend as positive, offering additional cash for accretive acquisitions that continue to expand Climb's network of customers.

Valuation

Today, Climb trades at 18x TTM GAAP earnings and 15x FWD GAAP earnings, leaving it about 30%-40% cheaper than its industry average, according to SA data. Morningstar gives a slightly lower industry average TTM P/E of 25, but even this would leave Climb 28% below-average. Given that revenue growth has trended in the mid-teens, well above the industry average of 10%, this discount seems somewhat questionable. Climb will, of course, trade at a discount due to its small size, complexity, and low liquidity, but even these don't seem to be enough to account for such a discount. By comparison, larger software distributors like TD Synnex and Ingram Micro trade at around 13-14x earnings, but have slower growth and less attractive unit economics than Climb. Looking at Climb's own history (in the chart below), we can see that it has now reverted back to its longer-term average multiples.

Technically, we can see how those longer-term valuation ranges have served as the bottom of a steep uptrend since 2019, during which time Climb's shares have appreciated at a ~32% CAGR. We might reasonably doubt whether shares can continue appreciating at that pace given revenue growth slowing down to around 10% and margin pressure.

Risks & Bottom Line

In short, Climb has sold off hard on margin compression over the last two quarters. If that deterioration continues, Climb's business will be in jeopardy, as their margins are already quite thin. Tough comps in Q4 made this even worse, with slowing billings growth relative to 2025. It will be important to watch the acceleration of Darktrace and Fortinet revenue over the next several quarters.

But in basic fundamental terms, the future still looks reasonably bright for Climb over the next several years. Vendor demand for their distribution services remains strong, and overall billings continue to grow. The key question going forward will be pricing, which in turn determines gross margins. At this point I think it is too early to tell what will happen in the SaaS marketplace, and so I am staying out of this stock.

data.title: Inflation Reports On Tap
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Fragile ceasefire: JD Vance will head to Pakistan for talks with Iran, while Tehran blocks the Strait of Hormuz as Israel ramps up attacks on Lebanon.

Tensions rise: The Trump administration is weighing a plan to relocate U.S. troops away from NATO countries that opposed the Iran war effort.

Blacklist fight: A federal appeals court refuses to halt the Pentagon's designation of Anthropic (ANTHRO) as a supply chain risk for now.

Data spotlight

Investors will get two major inflation reports this week, with the February PCE Price Index due today and the March Consumer Price Index tomorrow. The core versions of each index will be closely watched to assess underlying inflation trends, as the headline numbers, which include food and energy prices, tend to be more volatile.

What to expect: According to the Cleveland Federal Reserve's Inflation Nowcasting model, February's core PCE is seen rising 2.83% Y/Y, receding from 3.1% in January. Headline PCE is expected to increase 0.4% M/M vs. +0.3% in the prior month, according to consensus estimates. To note, the PCE data isn't all that fresh, as it was delayed due to the government shutdown last fall. March core CPI is estimated to rise 2.60% Y/Y, up from 2.46% in February, Inflation Nowcasting showed. Headline CPI for March is expected to rise 0.9% M/M from 0.3% in February, with the Y/Y pace rising to 3.4% from 2.4% in the prior month, according to consensus estimates.

Inflation outlook: Citi economist Veronica Clark points to several "conflicting and often unrelated factors" affecting core PCE — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — over the coming months. These include higher energy costs, slowing housing inflation, residual measurement issues from the government shutdown, falling equity prices, tariff effects on goods prices, and upward pressure on computer goods from surging AI demand. "After a few volatile months, we expect a clearer picture of slowing underlying inflation to emerge into the middle of the year, allowing Fed officials to resume rate cuts when the labor market data weakens further," Clark said. "But in the near term, market pricing for both cuts and the potential for hikes could be very sensitive to estimates of core PCE inflation."

FOMC minutes: Most Fed officials judged that progress toward the central bank's 2% inflation target could be slower than previously expected due to higher oil prices from the Iran conflict following the effects of tariffs imposed last year, according to the minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee meeting in March. They also saw that "the risk of inflation running persistently above the Committee's objective had increased." Overall, the officials said it's too early to see how the war would affect economic growth or monetary policy. Almost all participants voted to keep the federal funds rate target range at 3.50%-3.75%, with only one dissent. FOMC members did see the potential for rate cuts to resume, but likely later than some of them had originally anticipated.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Top 3 REITs For Reliable Income In Volatile Markets

Launching Main Street Alpha: Focusing On Long-Term Wealth Creation

I Can't Imagine Retiring On Dividends Without These Compounding Machines

Table-Pounding Fat Dividend Picks In This Fearful Market

The Stock Market Rally: Buy Or Fade It?

What else is happening...

Meta's (META) new Muse Spark model seen trailing AI leaders.

Pimco seeks to sell part of $14B debt for Oracle (ORCL) data center.

Visa (V) unveils AI agent-powered payment platform for businesses.

Morgan Stanley (MS) launches ETP tracking bitcoin performance.

Bessent calls on Congress to pass crypto bill amid regulatory gaps.

Treasury to talk private credit ratings with state insurance regulators.

Exxon (XOM) says 6% of its production shut in Q1 due to Iran war.

Valero's (VLO) Port Arthur refinery blast destroyed diesel hydrotreater.

RFK Jr. changes rules to pick CDC vaccine advisors after legal setback.

The long and winding road for GTA VI (TTWO) may finally be ending.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -0.7%. Hong Kong -0.5%. China -0.7%. India -1.2%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.3%. Paris -0.8%. Frankfurt -0.8%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.4%. S&P -0.3%. Nasdaq -0.2%. Crude +5% to $99.08. Gold -0.4% to $4,757.60. Bitcoin -0.1% to $71,369.
Ten-year Treasury Yield -1 bp to 4.29%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Byrna (BYRN) and WD-40 (WDFC).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Innodata: Still Not Chasing Despite Valuation Reset
data.description:
I have been covering Innodata (INOD) since June last year and have not budged from the Hold rating despite a stellar run from June to November 2025. Even in November, I had highlighted the higher-than defendable valuations as the key gating variable preventing a buy. I had also mentioned forward PS levels of ~4x as when things could be more workable. That has been breached, with Innodata correcting almost 33% since then. The purpose of this update is to review whether a below 4x PS valuation automatically qualifies Innodata as a buy. My analysis finds that several factors that were also part of the problem in November still persist - that is, revenue quality still looks uneven and model scalability questions are still unanswered. Hence, despite the valuation reset, I believe Innodata is still a hold. It is a better hold at these levels, but a buy thesis is not compelling enough yet.

Changes Since November

The biggest change since November is the valuation itself. The forward PS ratio was around 8x at that time, down from ~12x levels in the recent past but still looking expensive. That is now below the 4x mark I had indicated as a zone to consider a buy. Even a valuation of ~2.9x based on 2027 revenue expectations still looks higher than the sector median of ~1.8x. So, despite corrections, this is not outright cheap - at best it can be considered fair to reasonable for Innodata's high growth and AI exposure narrative.

In fact, the high-growth narrative has been further challenged since November, with Q4 2025 revenue growing only ~22% YoY, confirming that the slowdown seen in Q3 was not a one-off. Gross margins have also not shown any signs of breaking out above the 40% mark, levels that were broadly intact even in the pre-AI-narrative era.

The correction since November seems to be driven by a broader sell-off across enterprise software stocks. So, this is mostly multiple compression, rather than fundamental deterioration. For Innodata, the fact that I had highlighted in November seems to have been acknowledged now - that Innodata is a second order beneficiary of the AI tailwinds, and does not command platform-level economics. At the end of the day, it is still a human-intensive and services model with little scalability and leverage credentials, as the margins show. The high-growth aspect would have still helped, but that's not hypergrowth level anymore.

Key Concerns Remain

Management commentary in the Q4 2025 earnings call continues to cast doubts around the model's scalability ahead. There was talk around hiring ahead of demand, investments in data engineers and scientists - all pointing to building capacity to support future programs. This is not like a software model where revenue scales ahead of costs - not a new revelation, but it goes to show that the correction happened along with the rest of the software pack but for different reasons. While the enterprise software stocks sold off due to long duration uncertainties and threats from AI commoditizing several aspects of software functionalities, in the case of Innodata, we could be looking at a deeper problem around scalability and operating leverage. Those are not going away, and a rerating could only return if at least the high-growth narrative is upheld.

There was some commentary around automation and systems (across evaluation platforms, agent optimization pipelines, and adversarial simulation systems), but that appears to be addressing complexity rather than reducing headcount dependencies. The new tools and enhancements expand testing, validation, and data generation capabilities, but still require significant human oversight. Improvements in handling higher and more complex workload intensity could be a gain, but do not address operating leverage directly in my view.

For a company with a revenue run rate of ~$280m, $20m of workflows reported as deprecated and replaced by new programs highlights how the revenue is subject to churn and continuous reinvention, rather than steady compounding. The revenue model relies on adapting to new, evolving problem statements and shifting customer requirements that cannot be easily generalized or leveraged across use cases. That is a typical setup in a service-based business.

The reason I stop short of a sell on the basis of the structural limitations of the business, is that there are some legitimate positives that should be acknowledged, in terms of new growth vectors. Innodata is gaining traction across agentic AI workflows, robotics, and physical AI use cases. There are indications of an engagement with Palantir and forays into government or federal opportunities. But the impact cannot yet be quantified to be able to build a high conviction revenue acceleration thesis. Innodata's last couple of quarters at least do not show such traction translating into material growth acceleration - at a time when both Innodata's capabilities and AI demand should have already been at play. The Palantir engagement could be promising, but not necessarily one that impacts revenue sufficiently.

Waiting for Entry

The earlier Hold calls on Innodata were primarily defended through valuations. Now, the primary watchlist items for fresh entries shift to evidence of structural improvement. I will await gross margin improvement trends in Q1 2026 and beyond, toward 45-50%, to be able to say that automation and operating leverage are going hand in hand, at least to an extent. I will, more importantly, await any impact of the traction narrative (including the Palantir and federal inroads factor) on revenue. Over the past couple of quarters, we are seeing ~20% topline growth. Any new traction area that the management highlighted in Q4, like AI safety, should mean that the growth rate should revert toward 30% levels at least. That would tilt the combined revenue growth and FCF margin number decisively above 40. That benchmark is not really applicable to Innodata as a business, but its valuations at ~4x sales are what mid-sized SaaS names command (check Docusign and Twilio, for example).

At the moment, despite the valuation reset, Innodata seems to be still behind expectations in terms of growth and margins. Unless those start to show meaningful inflection from current levels, I still see Innodata as a hold. The positioning in a regime where Innodata can gain from AI tailwinds looks good, and traction appears to be picking up too. But those positives could already be in the price, unless we see a quantified impact of the new engagements discussed in Q4.

data.title: Bed Bath & Beyond Needs To Show How Its Strategy Can Work
data.description:
It's difficult to make a fundamental case for Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) stock at the moment. That's not to say there's no reason to buy the stock at all, but rather that the numbers, for now, don't provide much basis to do so.

To be fair, that's in large part because the numbers are somewhat fuzzy, particularly in the wake of recent deals to bring new brands (and new revenue) into the company. But at the same time, even pro forma for those deals, BB&B appears to be unprofitable on an adjusted EBITDA basis. There is some hope for improvement going forward, certainly, and enterprise value to revenue multiples are somewhat favorable. But those fundamentals in turn are based on the business actually performing better from here.

In other words, the investment case for BBBY centers not on the numbers right now, but rather on how strategy and execution set the company up for the future. And while that strategy, in particular, is intriguing (and innovative), neither seems like nearly enough to bet on the company or the stock at the moment.

The Numbers First

With BB&B bringing on both The Container Store and F9 Brands (including Lumber Liquidators), the current revenue base appears to be near $3 billion annually. In the fourth quarter shareholder letter, chief executive officer Marcus Lemonis said the revenue base, including the legacy Overstock business, Bed Bath & Beyond, buybuy BABY, and Kirkland's, was $1.5 billion. The announced acquisition this week of F9 Brands, whose portfolio is headlined by the formerly public Lumber Liquidators, brings in a little over $500 million in revenue. The Container Store's bankruptcy filing projected 2024 and 2025 revenue in the range of $800 million.

That gets the total to $2.9 billion or so. On a consolidated basis, the business still appears to be unprofitable, even on an adjusted EBITDA basis. BB&B lost $31 million in full-year fiscal 2025. And while Lemonis has said he sees upward of $40 million in savings from integrating the companies (even before the F9 deal), it seems likely that both F9 and The Container Store are adjusted EBITDA negative.

To be sure, that's a guess; The Container Store's bankruptcy filing did project positive adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2025, but the fact that BB&B didn't highlight positive EBITDA for the business may suggest that target wasn't reached. For F9, the existence of an earnout if the division generates just $20 million in adjusted EBITDA in any of the next five years suggests that business, too, remains unprofitable. ($20 million is a sub-4% margin, and five years is a long time.)

These numbers are a bit fuzzy; BB&B no doubt will give more information going forward. But even pro forma for synergies, the consolidated business is maybe at breakeven on an adjusted EBITDA basis. Meanwhile, pro forma market cap at Wednesday's close of $4.82 is right at $500 million. (Including shares at the end of 2025, plus consideration for the acquisitions, total share count seems to be about 104 million.) Net cash is about $90 million (accounting for cash paid and $40 million in financing inherited from the F9 deal), putting enterprise value at $410 million.

That does get EV/revenue to a seemingly attractive 0.14x. On its own, that figure might suggest some upside, given that specialty retailers nearly all get better multiples. But at the same time, Lemonis and BB&B have built a portfolio of brands that have struggled badly over the past decade (that includes Overstock as well), and there's likely some level of near-term cash burn even assuming the consolidated business begins a turnaround.

On the whole, a BBBY bull needs to assume something like 400-600 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. That would get EBITDA (assuming flattish revenue; that may be optimistic) toward the $100-$150 million range, at which point a 6-7x multiple would suggest BBBY stock could double from here.

Buying Retail In The 2020s

But the numbers simply point investors to the qualitative case here. Top-line trends need to improve, and margins need to expand. (In retail, of course, those usually happen concurrently.) And so investing in the new, combined, Bed Bath & Beyond remains a bet on improved execution and the core strategy.

Led by Lemonis, Bed Bath & Beyond has built a portfolio of brands that are intriguing, and acquired cheaply, but simply haven't performed well for some time. (That, of course, also includes the legacy Overstock business.) And in that context, a passage from the Container Store announcement stands out:

Today, the utilization of those assets [TCS has over 100 locations and more than 2.2 million square feet] falls below our expectations. We see a meaningful opportunity to better leverage these locations by expanding assortment, introducing additional brands, and creating a more comprehensive experience for the homeowner.

At its core, this argument is essentially the argument made by executives from (and shareholders in) specialty retailers during the 2010s. In those tellings, the reason why nearly all of those businesses were struggling was not secular change, but internal failings. The Container Store itself is a classic example. Across 2015 and 2016, the company moved aggressively into closets, saw its founder retire, and executed a wage freeze that completely contradicted a multi-decade strategy of "conscious capitalism". The argument at the time was that these massive changes would move the company back to the consistent growth needed to drive margin expansion and a higher share price. Over the next decade, TCS never really hit that bogey (despite some help around the pandemic), and bankruptcy eventually arrived last year.

Kirkland's tried turnarounds. They failed. Pier 1 liquidated in May 2020. Tuesday Morning filed Chapter 11 in 2023. Tile Shop Holdings (TTSH) never really established a turnaround. And the same, broader, issue held in categories like apparel as well. Execution was not the primary problem, nor was assortment. Rather, it was secular pressure on the entire category, driven of course by Amazon (AMZN) but larger, broader trends as well.

The 'Everything Home' Plan

Lemonis's enormously aggressive strategy to create an "Everything Home ecosystem" rests in large part on these businesses getting turned around. They are the first pillar—and, at the moment, essentially the only pillar that actually exists—in a strategy to provide homeowners basically everything they need. Pillar Two will move the company into insurance, warranties, and mortgages. Home services and installation will follow.

But that entire strategy requires the underlying retail businesses to improve. (Some kind of real economic activity in the blockchain businesses tZERO and GrainChain would help as well.) And that in turn requires that Bed Bath & Beyond prove that it can drive growth in businesses that, across the board, have failed to drive growth for essentially a decade now.

And, again, the argument when those struggles began in many cases was that the problems were more self-inflicted than external. Looking back, it is exceptionally difficult (in fact, close to impossible) to believe that argument was correct. It was secular changes, specifically around e-commerce, that undercut the value of brick-and-mortar networks. (For its part, Lumber Liquidators simply wasn't good enough to compete in its industry, but the scale of Home Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW) obviously made its job much harder.)

And so what Lemonis is trying to do is essentially fight a trend that has been clearly established. It's possible, perhaps, to argue that Bed Bath & Beyond can be the exception to the rule. Authentic Brands Group has shown the potential value in bankrupt/distressed brands (though it operates a much simpler and asset-light licensing model, with intellectual property as well). Blockchain efforts might help (though there, too, BB&B has a lot to prove).

Still, at the end of the day, investors in BBBY stock are betting on Lemonis to buck a massive, secular trend. Importantly, they are betting on him to do so with brands that, to be blunt, are faded.

It's possible that Lemonis can pull it off. But at the same time, it is strange to promise, for instance, "the acquisition or development of a scaled residential brokerage network of tens of thousands of agents" at the same time the underlying business is posting declining revenue and orders. There is a sense that Lemonis and his company are trying to sprint before they can walk.

And, at the end of the day, one has to wonder whether betting on Lemonis is the right play, too. The building of Camping World Holdings (CWH) admittedly was impressive (though Lemonis received an assist from famed auto executive Lee Iacocca). But this is simply a different animal, and certainly, judging by the BBBY stock price, Lemonis's involvement to this point hasn't helped. For that to change, Lemonis and Bed Bath & Beyond need to start showing that they can do something that for most retailers—including its new brands—has been impossible.
